articles.title: RADAR-X: A Robust Adversarial-Resilient Dual-Stream Network Intrusion Detection Framework with Explainable AI and ATT&CK-Aligned SOC Integration
articles.abstract: Network intrusion detection systems (NIDS) built upon deep learning architectures have demonstrated strong performance on benchmark datasets; however, they remain critically vulnerable to adversarial perturbation attacks, exhibit poor calibration and explainability, and lack operational integration with enterprise Security Operations Center (SOC) workflows. This paper presents RADAR-X (Robust Adversarial Dual-stream Adaptive Representation eXplainer), a novel framework that addresses these three interdependent limitations simultaneously. RADAR-X integrates a dual-stream encoder combining a Transformer-based temporal attention module with a Graph Neural Network (GNN)-based topological feature extractor, a certified adversarial robustness layer employing TRADES regularization and randomized smoothing, a SHAP-based explainability engine, and a calibrated alert scoring mechanism aligned with MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques. The proposed evaluation design employs three public benchmark datasets (CICIDS-2017, UNSW-NB15, and NSD-KDD) augmented with five adversarial attack scenarios (FGSM, PGD-40, C&W, DeepFool, and a novel Constraint-Aware Black-Box attack). Proposed experimental projections indicate F1-scores in the range of 0.961–0.978, ROC-AUC values between 0.982 and 0.991, false positive rates below 0.42%, and adversarial robustness retaining F1 > 0.893 under PGD-40 attacks—representing an estimated 12–18 percentage point improvement over the strongest prior baseline under adversarial conditions. RADAR-X is designed for deployment-realistic latency (< 8 ms per sample) and demonstrates measurable SOC applicability through ATT&CK tactic classification accuracy of 0.944. This work establishes a new methodological standard for adversarially robust, operationally deployable, and explainable network intrusion detection.

articles.title: Carbon dioxide sequestration into biomineral armor by ants
articles.abstract: Over geologic time, Earth’s climate has been shaped by the capture and conversion of atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO2) into stable carbonate minerals, including dolomite [CaMg(CO3)2]. Accelerating natural carbon mineralization offers significant potential for mitigating anthropogenic climate change. Using stable carbon isotope tracking, nano-scale secondary ion mass spectrometry, and 13C solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy, we show that, paralleling global biosphere-level processes, Sericomyrmex amabilis fungus-farming ants rapidly convert CO2 in their nest chambers into a biomineral layer covering their exoskeletons. We further reveal that biogenic carbon mineralization by these ants produces partially ordered dolomite that significantly hardens the exoskeleton, representing a previously unappreciated example of biomineralizing dolomite achieved under ambient conditions that challenge synthetic approaches. This rapid sequestration of CO2 with 60–86 µg per ant (up to 7% body dry weight) into defensive armor provides a fascinating natural system of low-energy-cost mediation of potentially toxic accumulation of atmospheric CO2 that could inform human efforts to mitigate climate change.
